subject: ap-precalculus
theme: Exponential and logarithmic functions model multiplicative growth and decay, and their inverse relationship allows real-world quantities like population, money, and radioactive material to be analyzed and predicted.

core_concepts.title: Exponential Growth and Decay
core_concepts.detail: An exponential function $f(x) = a \cdot b^x$ has a constant ratio: each equal increase in $x$ multiplies the output by the base $b$. If $b > 1$, the function grows; if $0 < b < 1$, it decays. The initial value is $a = f(0)$. Crucially, for equal input changes $\Delta x$, exponential output RATIOS are constant (not differences) — this is the rate-of-change signature that distinguishes exponential from linear behavior (LO 2.2.B).

core_concepts.title: The Natural Base $e$
core_concepts.detail: The natural base $e \approx 2.718$ arises naturally in continuous processes. Continuous exponential growth is modeled by $f(x) = ae^{rx}$, where $r > 0$ means growth and $r < 0$ means decay.

core_concepts.title: Logarithms as Inverses
core_concepts.detail: Logarithms are the inverse of exponentials: $\log_b(x) = y$ means $b^y = x$. The natural log $\ln(x) = \log_e(x)$ is the inverse of $e^x$. Key identities: $\ln(e^x) = x$ and $e^{\ln x} = x$.

core_concepts.title: Logarithm Properties
core_concepts.detail: The three core log rules let you rewrite expressions: $\log_b(MN) = \log_b M + \log_b N$ (product rule), $\log_b\!\left(\frac{M}{N}\right) = \log_b M - \log_b N$ (quotient rule), and $\log_b(M^p) = p\log_b M$ (power rule).

core_concepts.title: Change of Base Formula
core_concepts.detail: The change of base formula $\log_b a = \frac{\ln a}{\ln b}$ converts any logarithm to base $e$ or base 10 for calculator evaluation. It is essential for solving exponential equations with bases other than $e$ or 10.

core_concepts.title: Transformations and Translation Equivalence
core_concepts.detail: Transformations of $f(x) = b^x$: adding $k$ to the output shifts the horizontal asymptote from $y = 0$ to $y = k$; replacing $x$ with $-x$ converts growth to decay. A key equivalence (CED 2.4.A): a horizontal shift by $k$ is identical to a vertical dilation — $f(x + k) = b^k \cdot f(x)$. For example, $2^{x+3} = 8 \cdot 2^x$. This means every horizontal translation of an exponential can be rewritten as a change in initial value.

core_concepts.title: Solving Exponential and Log Equations
core_concepts.detail: To solve an exponential equation, isolate the exponential and take the natural log of both sides. To solve a logarithmic equation, isolate the log and exponentiate both sides — always check for extraneous solutions since $\log_b(x)$ requires $x > 0$. For compositions involving logarithms, such as $\ln(g(x))$, the domain requires $g(x) > 0$; the composition inherits the inner function's domain restriction.

core_concepts.title: Competing Model Selection
core_concepts.detail: To determine whether data follows an exponential or linear model, examine equal-interval behavior: if consecutive output RATIOS over equal input intervals are constant, the model is exponential; if consecutive output DIFFERENCES are constant, the model is linear (LO 2.6.A). Semi-log linearization provides a formal check: plot $\ln(y)$ vs. $x$ — a straight line confirms an exponential model with slope $\ln b$ and intercept $\ln a$.

formulas.name: General Exponential Function
formulas.description: Models multiplicative growth or decay. The initial value a gives f(0), and the base b is the growth factor per unit of x. If b > 1, the function grows; if 0 < b < 1, it decays.
formulas.example: A population of 500 growing by 8% per year: $P(t) = 500 \cdot (1.08)^t$. After 10 years: $P(10) = 500 \cdot (1.08)^{10} \approx 1079$.

formulas.name: Horizontal Translation Equivalence
formulas.description: Shifting an exponential function horizontally by k units produces the same result as multiplying the entire function by b^k — a vertical dilation. This means a horizontal shift changes only the initial value, not the base.
formulas.example: Let $f(x) = 5 \cdot 2^x$. Then $f(x + 3) = 5 \cdot 2^{x+3} = 5 \cdot 2^3 \cdot 2^x = 40 \cdot 2^x$. So shifting $f$ left by 3 is the same as multiplying the initial value by $2^3 = 8$.

formulas.name: Composition of Functions
formulas.description: Applying function g first, then feeding its output into function f. The inner function g runs first; the outer function f runs on the result. The domain of the composition must satisfy the domain constraints of both functions.
formulas.example: Let $f(x) = \ln(x)$ and $g(x) = x^2 + 1$. Then $(f \circ g)(x) = \ln(x^2 + 1)$. Since $x^2 + 1 \geq 1 > 0$ for all real $x$, the domain is all real numbers — no restrictions needed. If instead $g(x) = x^2 - 1$, then $\ln(x^2 - 1)$ requires $x^2 - 1 > 0$, so $|x| > 1$.

formulas.name: Inverse Function
formulas.description: The inverse function reverses the input-output relationship of the original. If (a, b) is on the graph of f, then (b, a) is on the graph of f^{-1}. Graphically, f^{-1} is the reflection of f over the line y = x.
formulas.example: Find the inverse of $f(x) = 5 \cdot 2^x$. Set $y = 5 \cdot 2^x$, solve for $x$: $\frac{y}{5} = 2^x$, so $x = \log_2\!\left(\frac{y}{5}\right) = \frac{\ln(y/5)}{\ln 2}$. Thus $f^{-1}(x) = \frac{\ln(x/5)}{\ln 2}$. Domain of $f^{-1}$: $x > 0$ (since you can only take $\ln$ of positive numbers).

formulas.name: Change of Base Formula
formulas.description: Converts any logarithm to base e or base 10 for calculator evaluation. Also used to rewrite exponential equations in solvable form when the base is not e or 10.

formulas.name: Half-Life Model
formulas.description: Models radioactive decay or any quantity that halves every h units of time. After each half-life period h, exactly half the original amount remains.
formulas.example: Carbon-14 has half-life $h = 5730$ years. Starting with 100 grams: $A(5730) = 100 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^1 = 50$ grams.

formulas.name: Logarithm Properties
formulas.katex_string: \log_b(MN) = \log_b M + \log_b N; \quad \log_b\!\left(\frac{M}{N}\right) = \log_b M - \log_b N; \quad \log_b(M^p) = p\log_b M
formulas.description: The three core log rules: logs convert multiplication to addition, division to subtraction, and exponents to multiplication. Use these to expand or condense logarithmic expressions before solving.

formulas.name: Semi-Log Linearization
formulas.description: Taking the natural log of an exponential model produces a linear equation in x. This lets you confirm exponential behavior (a straight line on a ln-y vs x plot) and recover the base and initial value from the slope and intercept.
formulas.example: A ln(P) vs. t scatter plot shows slope $0.6$ and y-intercept $2.3$. From $\ln(P) = 0.6t + 2.3$: the base is $b = e^{0.6} \approx 1.822$ and the initial value is $a = e^{2.3} \approx 9.97$, so $P(t) \approx 9.97 \cdot e^{0.6t}$.

exam_tip: The AP exam frequently presents exponential models in table form — identify the constant ratio (consecutive outputs divide to give the same factor) to confirm exponential behavior, then use $f(x) = ab^x$ with two data points to find $a$ and $b$. When solving for an exponent in word problems, always isolate the exponential term by dividing by the initial value before taking the logarithm — skipping this step is the most common source of algebra errors.
